original_abstract: Pushing data traffic from cellular to WiFi is an example of inter radio access technology (RAT) offloading. While this clearly alleviates congestion on the over-loaded cellular network, the ultimate potential of such offloading and its effect on overall system performance is not well understood. To address this, we develop a general and tractable model that consists of $M$ different RATs, each deploying up to $K$ different tiers of access points (APs), where each tier differs in transmit power, path loss exponent, deployment density and bandwidth. Each class of APs is modeled as an independent Poisson point process (PPP), with mobile user locations modeled as another independent PPP, all channels further consisting of i.i.d. Rayleigh fading. The distribution of rate over the entire network is then derived for a weighted association strategy, where such weights can be tuned to optimize a particular objective. We show that the optimum fraction of traffic offloaded to maximize $\SINR$ coverage is not in general the same as the one that maximizes rate coverage, defined as the fraction of users achieving a given rate.
humanized_abstract: As wireless data growth continues to outpace network capacity, Mobile Network Operators (MNOs) are increasingly exploring means of managing congestion. One of the most widely adopted practices is pushing data traffic from cellular to WiFi, referred to as inter radio access technology (RAT) offloading. While offloading the increasingly congested cellular network does provide some benefits, there is limited understanding of the long term implications of such practices and their overall system performance. In this work, we develop a general yet tractable model comprising $M$ different RATs, each having up to $K$ different tiers of access points (APs). Each tier is characterized by a unique transmit power, path loss exponent, deployment density, and bandwidth. We model each class of APs using an independent Poisson point process (PPP) and mobile user locations using another independent PPP. Furthermore, all channels are characterized by i.i.d. Rayleigh fading. We derive the distribution of rates in the network and evaluate a weighted association strategy with tunable weights that can be optimized to achieve various objectives. We find that the optimum fraction of traffic to be offloaded in order to maximize $\SINR$ coverage is not necessarily the same as the optimum fraction of traffic to be offloaded in order to maximize the rate coverage, i.e., the fraction of users that achieve a given rate.
